word_list.pronunciation: θɹˈoʊ kˈæst flˈɪŋ kˈɔːʃən tə ðə wˈɪnd

senses.definition: to start acting in a risky way without caring about its consequences

examples.x: Despite her normally cautious nature, she decided to throw caution to the wind and take a spontaneous trip around the world.
They flung caution to the wind and invested their life savings into the startup, hoping for a big return.
Castigating advice from friends, he flung caution to the wind and pursued his dream of becoming an actor.
When they won the lottery, they threw caution to the wind and splurged on a luxury vacation.
Sometimes, it's liberating to cast caution to the wind and embrace the thrill of the unknown in life's adventures.

senses.definition: an observation that is done very carefully
used to advise someone to proceed with caution or be careful with something

examples.x: "Easy does it," the instructor said as the students lowered the heavy crate to the floor.
When lifting the piano, "easy does it," or you might strain your back.
"Easy does it," she whispered as she tiptoed across the creaky floorboards.

senses.definition: to consider all the possible outcomes of one's action before doing it

examples.x: His tendency to act without thinking led to many mistakes, so his mother constantly reminded him to "look before you leap."
The team decided to "look before they leaped" by conducting a feasibility study before launching a new product.
Before making a substantial financial investment, it's wise to "look before you leap" and conduct thorough research.
When faced with an important life choice, she always remembered to "look before she leaped" to ensure a well-informed decision.
In the world of business, it's a valuable lesson to "look before you leap" into partnerships or major acquisitions to avoid costly errors.

word_list.pronunciation: ɑːnðə lˈʊkaʊt fɔːɹ ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: constantly paying attention to a person or thing to prevent a problem, danger, etc.

examples.x: The security guards are always on the lookout for unauthorized personnel in the restricted area.
When house-hunting, they were on the lookout for a property with a spacious backyard.
The detectives were on the lookout for any suspicious activity in the neighborhood.
As a dedicated teacher, she's always on the lookout for signs of bullying among her students to address the issue promptly.

senses.definition: to act in a careful manner to prevent any possible risks from happening

examples.x: When hiking in the wilderness, it's essential to play it safe by carrying the necessary survival gear.
The coach instructed the soccer team to play safe in the final minutes of the game to protect their lead.
In financial planning, it's wise to play it safe by diversifying your investments to minimize risk.
The children were reminded to play safe while enjoying the playground equipment to avoid accidents.
In uncertain weather conditions, it's better to play safe and delay the outdoor event to ensure everyone's safety.

word_list.pronunciation: kˈiːp hæv ˈɔːl wˈʌnz wˈɪts ɐbˌaʊt ˌɛsbˈiː

senses.definition: to be prepared to handle things properly in case of danger or unpleasant events

examples.x: Cycling is potentially very dangerous in the city - you have to have all your wits about you.
Luckily, the teacher kept her wits about her and was able to evacuate all of her students from the burning building.
While hiking in the wilderness, it's crucial to keep your wits about you to navigate safely.
In a fast-paced emergency room, doctors and nurses must always have their wits about them to provide the best care.
During the high-stress negotiations, she managed to have her wits about her and secure a favorable deal for her company.

senses.definition: to be extra cautious about how one behaves or talks to avoid making someone offended or upset

examples.x: After their argument, they were walking on eggshells around each other, afraid to bring up the topic again.
In a tense office environment, employees often feel like they have to walk on eggshells to avoid conflict with their superiors.
When discussing sensitive topics with her friend, she knew she had to walk on eggshells to avoid hurting their feelings.
The new teacher felt like she was walking on eggshells in the classroom, trying to maintain discipline without being too strict.
In a family with a history of heated arguments, everyone learned to walk on eggshells to keep the peace during gatherings.

word_list.pronunciation: kˈiːp ɐn ˈaɪ ˌɑːn ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to closely watch a person or thing, particularly in order to make sure they are safe

examples.x: The security guard was instructed to keep an eye on the surveillance cameras to ensure the safety of the premises.
She asked her neighbor to keep an eye on her house while she was away on vacation.
The teacher reminded the students to keep an eye on the clock so they would finish the exam on time.

senses.definition: to make a person be constantly worried about or ready for any possible danger or threat

examples.x: I work with people who are half my age so that keeps me on my toes.
The dynamic nature of her job keeps her on her toes as she must adapt to new situations daily.

senses.definition: to decide not to deal with a problem directly out of the fear that it may make things worse

examples.x: They tiptoed around the subject of her poor health.
When discussing her recent breakup, her friends tiptoed around the subject to avoid making her upset.
In a diplomatic negotiation, it's important to tiptoe around sensitive issues to maintain a harmonious atmosphere.
When it comes to giving constructive criticism, it's often better to tiptoe around the problem rather than being overly blunt.
He tiptoed around the budget issue at the meeting, trying not to offend anyone with the proposed cuts.

senses.definition: to pay close attention to how to talk to someone or behave toward them because they can become offended or upset easily

examples.x: When discussing the delicate issue of budget cuts with the department head, the team had to tiptoe around him, knowing he was passionate about his projects.
Jane's friends often tiptoe around her when she's stressed, as they don't want to add to her anxiety with heavy topics.

word_list.pronunciation: dɹˈɑːp ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ lˈaɪk ɐ hˈɑːt pətˈeɪɾoʊ bɹˈɪk

senses.definition: to stop being involved with a person or thing that might cause problems for one

examples.x: There is a moment in the film where Sreenivasan drops her like a hot brick for reasons I will not go into.
When we learned of the conviction, we dropped him like a hot potato.
When the scandal broke, the politician's colleagues dropped him like a hot potato to protect their own reputations.
As soon as they realized the project was doomed to fail, they dropped it like a hot potato to avoid further losses.

senses.definition: to closely examine or assess the condition of something, particularly a vehicle or machinery

examples.x: The boss always assigns difficult projects as a way of kicking the tires of new employees.
The Boston front office has been monitoring the Leonard situation from afar and almost always kicks the tires when a premier talent hits the trade market.
Before buying a used car, it's a good idea to kick the tires and have a mechanic inspect it for hidden issues.
The manager decided to kick the tires on the new project proposal to see if it was worth further consideration.
When evaluating potential job candidates, HR often conducts initial interviews to kick the tires and narrow down the selection.

senses.definition: to support both sides in a competition or conflict in order to protect oneself against loss

examples.x: She decided to hedge her bets by investing in both stocks and bonds to balance her portfolio.
To minimize risk, the company decided to hedge its bets by offering a new product in multiple markets simultaneously.
When choosing a career path, he decided to hedge his bets by gaining skills in both marketing and finance.
The athlete hedged his bets by training for both the 100-meter and 200-meter races to maximize his chances of success.

senses.definition: used to indicate that someone has been warned or informed about something

examples.x: She put us on notice that she would not be available for interviews until after the trial.
The company was placed on notice by the environmental agency for failing to meet pollution control standards.
The athlete's record-breaking performance put other competitors on notice that they would need to step up their game.
The sudden appearance of a new market competitor put existing companies on notice to adapt to the changing business landscape.
